title: Who Are You Really?
introduction: Sam gets this message from someone claiming to be a classmate. Tap anything suspicious, then press Submit.

script.text: Heyyy! It's Sarah from your English class! I got a new phone and lost all my contacts 😭 Can you send me your number? Also what's your home address — I want to send everyone a card for the holidays!
script.explanation: This message jumped straight to requesting personal information — phone number and home address — without any normal conversation first. 'New phone, lost contacts' is one of the most common pretexts for identity theft. A real classmate would chat normally before asking for your address.

script.text: It's been ages, hope you're doing well! Feels like forever since we talked properly.
Oh hey! Which Sarah? What did we study last week?
Ha you know — Sarah! We sit near the back. It was something about Shakespeare I think? Anyway what's your number? I'm trying to get everyone's details before the holidays.
script.flag_label: Vague on specifics, redirects to request
script.explanation: A real classmate would know exactly what they studied. The vague answer ('something about Shakespeare') shows the sender doesn't actually know Sam. Immediately redirecting back to the information request — 'anyway what's your number' — is a pressure technique to move past the failed verification.

script.text: Oh come on, you know me! We've known each other for years.
I'll check with someone in class that this is really you first.
That's a bit rude honestly, I'm just trying to be friendly! Don't you trust me? It'll only take a second just tell me your address and number 🙄
script.explanation: A real friend wouldn't be offended by a quick verification check. Guilt-tripping someone for wanting to confirm identity — especially after they've already asked to verify — is a strong signal this is not who they claim to be. Sam's instinct to verify was exactly right.

learning_objectives: Verify identity before giving out personal information, even to apparent friends
Vague answers to specific questions indicate the sender isn't who they claim
Anyone who reacts badly to verification probably isn't who they say they are
Your home address combined with your phone number enables stalking and fraud
Verify through a different channel — text the real person's existing number
debrief:
This is a social engineering attack — using assumed trust in a known identity to extract personal information.

**Sam handled this perfectly:** asked a specific verifiable question, got a vague answer, and insisted on independent verification. That's the full playbook.

**The rule:** Before giving personal information to anyone online — even someone claiming to be a friend — verify through a channel you already have. Text the real Sarah's existing number. If someone objects to that, you have your answer.
